problem:
There are $2026$ integers greater than $1$ written on a blackboard, not necessarily all different. In a move, Confucius chooses two integers $m>1$ and $n>1$ from different places on the blackboard and replaces them with
\[
  \gcd(m,n)\qquad\text{and}\qquad
  \frac{\operatorname{lcm}(m,n)}{\gcd(m,n)}.
\]
He continues to make moves while it is possible to do so.
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(a)] Prove that, regardless of Confucius's choices, after finitely many moves exactly one integer $M$ on the blackboard is greater than $1$.
\item[(b)] Prove that the value of $M$ does not depend on Confucius's choices.
\end{enumerate}
reference_solution:
Write $d=\gcd(m,n)$. The product of the two selected entries changes from $mn$ to
\[
 d\cdot \frac{\operatorname{lcm}(m,n)}{d}
 =\operatorname{lcm}(m,n)=\frac{mn}{d}.
\]
Let $r$ be the number of entries greater than $1$, and let $P$ be the product of all
entries. If $d=1$, then $P$ is unchanged while $r$ decreases by one. If $d>1$, then
$P$ decreases strictly and $r$ cannot increase. Thus the ordered pair $(r,P)$ decreases
lexicographically after every move. Since lexicographic order on pairs of positive
integers is well-founded, the process terminates.

Every legal move leaves at least one of its two outputs greater than $1$. Consequently,
the process cannot end with all entries equal to $1$. At termination there cannot be
two entries greater than $1$, since then another move would be legal. This proves (a).

For (b), fix a prime $p$. If the current entries are $t_1,\dots,t_{2026}$, consider
\[
 I_p=\gcd\bigl(\nu_p(t_1),\dots,\nu_p(t_{2026})\bigr),
\]
using the convention that the gcd of a list of zeros is $0$. Suppose the selected
entries have $p$-adic valuations $x\le y$. Their two new valuations are
\[
 x\qquad\text{and}\qquad y-x.
\]
Since
\[
 \gcd(x,y-x)=\gcd(x,y),
\]
the quantity $I_p$ is invariant under every move. In the final position all entries
except $M$ are $1$, and hence
\[
 \nu_p(M)=I_p
 =\gcd\bigl(\nu_p(a_1),\dots,\nu_p(a_{2026})\bigr),
\]
where $a_1,\dots,a_{2026}$ are the initial entries. This determines the exponent of
every prime in $M$, so $M$ is uniquely determined by the initial board.

grading_scheme.title: Proving finite termination
grading_scheme.desc: For giving a well-founded monovariant and applying it rigorously. A standard full-credit argument lets $r$ be the number of entries greater than $1$ and $P$ the product of all entries: if $d=\gcd(m,n)=1$, then $P$ is unchanged and $r$ decreases; if $d>1$, then $P$ decreases and $r$ cannot increase. Thus $(r,P)$ decreases lexicographically. Award $1$ of the $2$ points for identifying the two monotone quantities but not fully ruling out an infinite sequence, or for an equivalent monovariant with a minor justification gap.

grading_scheme.title: Showing exactly one non-unit remains
grading_scheme.desc: For proving both sides of the terminal-state claim: at termination there are at most one entry greater than $1$, while there cannot be zero because every legal move leaves at least one of its two outputs greater than $1$.

grading_scheme.title: Identifying a prime-valuation invariant
grading_scheme.desc: For fixing a prime $p$ and proposing the invariant $I_p=\gcd(\nu_p(t_1),\ldots,\nu_p(t_{2026}))$, or an equivalent invariant that determines the exponent of $p$ in the final number.

grading_scheme.title: Proving the valuation invariant
grading_scheme.desc: For showing that if the selected entries have $p$-adic valuations $x\le y$, then the new valuations are $x$ and $y-x$, and using $\gcd(x,y-x)=\gcd(x,y)$ to prove that $I_p$ is unchanged. Award $1$ of the $2$ points for the correct valuation update with the global invariance conclusion incomplete.

grading_scheme.title: Determining the final value of $M$
grading_scheme.desc: For deducing $\nu_p(M)=\gcd_i\nu_p(a_i)$ for every prime $p$ and concluding that these exponents uniquely determine $M$, hence make it independent of all choices.

problem:
Let $ABC$ be a triangle, and let $M$ and $N$ be the midpoints of sides $AB$ and $AC$, respectively. Let $K$ and $L$ be chosen strictly inside triangles $BMC$ and $BNC$, respectively, such that $K$ lies strictly inside $\angle LBA$, $L$ lies strictly inside $\angle ACK$, and
\[
  \angle KBA=\angle ACL,\qquad
  \angle LBK=\angle LNC,\qquad
  \angle LCK=\angle BMK.
\]
Let $O$ be the circumcenter of triangle $AKL$. Prove that $OM=ON$.
reference_solution:
We use directed angles modulo $180^\circ$ and directed products of collinear lengths.
Put
\[
 X=BK\cap AC,
 \qquad
 Y=CL\cap AB.
\]
The three angle conditions give, respectively, the cyclic quadrilaterals
\[
 B,Y,X,C;\qquad X,N,L,B;\qquad Y,M,K,C.
\]
For example, the first condition is
$\angle XBY=\angle XCY$; the other two are read in the same way from the defining
collinearities. Because $MN\parallel BC$, the first cyclicity also gives that
$M,Y,X,N$ are cyclic. Denote the circles through $X,N,L,B$ and $Y,M,K,C$ by
$\omega_B$ and $\omega_C$, respectively.

Let the lines $XK$ and $XC$ meet $\omega_C$ again at $K'$ and $C'$. Similarly,
let $YL$ and $YB$ meet $\omega_B$ again at $L'$ and $B'$. We first locate $B'$ and
$C'$. Taking powers of $A$ with respect to $\omega_C$ and to the circle $(MYXN)$ gives
\[
 \overline{AC'}\,\overline{AC}
 =\overline{AY}\,\overline{AM}
 =\overline{AX}\,\overline{AN}
 =\overline{AX}\cdot\frac{\overline{AC}}2.
\]
Thus $\overline{AC'}=\overline{AX}/2$, so $C'$ is the midpoint of $AX$. In the same
way, $B'$ is the midpoint of $AY$.

Let $E$ and $F$ be the midpoints of $BY$ and $CX$, respectively. We claim that
\[
 A,K,L,E,F,K',L'
\]
lie on one circle. Indeed, power of $X$ with respect to $\omega_C$ and the midpoint
relations give
\[
 \overline{XK}\,\overline{XK'}
 =\overline{XC'}\,\overline{XC}
 =\overline{XA}\,\overline{XF},
\]
so $A,K,F,K'$ are cyclic. Also, taking power of $B$ with respect to $\omega_C$,
\[
 \overline{BK}\,\overline{BK'}
 =\overline{BM}\,\overline{BY}
 =\overline{BA}\,\overline{BE},
\]
so $A,E,K,K'$ are cyclic. Hence $K$ and $K'$ lie on the circumcircle of $AEF$.
The symmetric argument with $\omega_B$ places $L$ and $L'$ on the same circle.
In particular, it is the circumcircle of $AKL$; let its radius be $\rho$.

It remains to compare the powers of $M$ and $N$. Since $E$ is the midpoint of $BY$
and $M$ is the midpoint of $BA$, we have
$\overline{ME}=\overline{AY}/2$. Similarly,
$\overline{NF}=\overline{AX}/2$. Using the cyclic quadrilateral $MYXN$ and reversing
the two directed segments that point toward $A$,
\[
 \overline{MA}\,\overline{ME}
 =-\frac12\overline{AM}\,\overline{AY}
 =-\frac12\overline{AN}\,\overline{AX}
 =\overline{NA}\,\overline{NF}.
\]
These are precisely the powers of $M$ and $N$ with respect to the circumcircle of
$AKL$. Since its center is $O$,
\[
 OM^2-\rho^2=ON^2-\rho^2,
\]
and therefore $OM=ON$.

grading_scheme.title: Introducing the cyclic framework
grading_scheme.desc: For defining $X=BK\cap AC$ and $Y=CL\cap AB$ (or equivalent auxiliary points) and correctly translating the angle conditions into the cyclic quadrilaterals $BYXC$, $XNLB$, and $YMKC$, with $MYXN$ cyclic as a consequence of $MN\parallel BC$.

grading_scheme.title: Locating the second intersections
grading_scheme.desc: For defining the second intersections $K\prime,C\prime$ on $(YMKC)$ and $L\prime,B\prime$ on $(XNLB)$ and proving, with a consistent directed-length convention or justified point order, that $C\prime$ and $B\prime$ are the midpoints of $AX$ and $AY$, respectively.

grading_scheme.title: Identifying the circumcircle of $AKL$
grading_scheme.desc: For taking $E$ and $F$ as the midpoints of $BY$ and $CX$ and proving, through identities such as $XK\cdot XK\prime=XA\cdot XF$ and $BK\cdot BK\prime=BA\cdot BE$, that $A,E,F,K,K\prime$ are concyclic and, symmetrically, that $L,L\prime$ lie on the same circle. Award $1$ of the $2$ points for completing only one side or for establishing a substantial but incomplete route to $E,F\in(AKL)$.

grading_scheme.title: Establishing equal powers of $M$ and $N$
grading_scheme.desc: For using $\overline{ME}=\overline{AY}/2$, $\overline{NF}=\overline{AX}/2$, and the cyclicity of $MYXN$ to obtain the directed power identity $\overline{MA}\,\overline{ME}=-\tfrac12\overline{AM}\,\overline{AY}=-\tfrac12\overline{AN}\,\overline{AX}=\overline{NA}\,\overline{NF}$, or an equivalent correctly signed identity, and to interpret it as equality of the powers of $M$ and $N$ with respect to $(AKL)$. Award $1$ of the $2$ points for a correct unsigned/product relation whose sign or power-of-a-point interpretation remains incomplete.

grading_scheme.title: Concluding $OM=ON$
grading_scheme.desc: For, after establishing that $M$ and $N$ have equal powers with respect to the circumcircle of $AKL$ (or independently re-establishing an equivalent decisive relation), writing $\operatorname{Pow}(M)=OM^2-\rho^2$ and $\operatorname{Pow}(N)=ON^2-\rho^2$ for the common circumradius $\rho$, and concluding $OM=ON$. Merely citing the center--power formula without the equal-power input does not earn this point.

problem: Let $n$ be a positive integer. Liu Bang and Xiang Yu have a stick of length $1$ and want to divide it between themselves. Liu Bang marks at most $n$ points on the stick, and then Xiang Yu marks at most $n$ points on the stick; all marked points are distinct. The stick is cut at all marked points. Afterwards, they take turns claiming any unclaimed piece, with Liu Bang going first. Each player seeks to maximize the total length of his own pieces. Determine the largest number $c_n$ that Liu Bang can guarantee, regardless of Xiang Yu's play.
reference_solution:
The answer is
\[
 \boxed{c_n=\frac{2^n}{2^{n+1}-1}}.
\]
For a sorted finite list $z_1\ge z_2\ge\cdots\ge z_m\ge0$, the value of the drafting
game in score difference for the player to move is
\[
 z_1-z_2+z_3-\cdots+(-1)^{m+1}z_m.
 \tag{1}
\]
This follows by induction on $m$: taking $z_1$ attains the displayed value, while if
one takes $z_j$ instead, the induction hypothesis for the remaining list shows that
the loss relative to taking $z_1$ is twice a sum of nonnegative adjacent differences
$z_1-z_2,z_3-z_4,\dots$. Thus taking a largest remaining piece is optimal at every turn.

If Liu Bang makes fewer than $n$ effective cuts, he creates at most $n$ positive
segments. Xiang Yu can bisect every one of them, using at most $n$ marks, and force
a score difference of $0$. Thus, for the upper bound, we may assume Liu Bang makes
exactly $n$ effective cuts and creates $n+1$ positive original segments. If Xiang Yu
uses fewer than $n$ marks, pad the final list with zero-length pieces for bookkeeping;
this does not change either score. We may therefore list $2n+1$ lengths in
nonincreasing order:
\[
 x_1\ge x_2\ge\cdots\ge x_{2n+1}\ge0.
\]
By (1), Liu Bang receives $x_1+x_3+\cdots+x_{2n+1}$. Define the score difference
\[
 G=x_1-x_2+x_3-x_4+\cdots+x_{2n+1}.
\]
Since the two players' total lengths sum to $1$, Liu Bang's score is $(1+G)/2$.
It is therefore enough to prove that the value of $G$ is
\[
 \delta_n:=\frac{1}{2^{n+1}-1}.
\]

\medskip
\noindent\textbf{Xiang Yu can force $G\le\delta_n$.}
Suppose Liu Bang's $n$ cuts produce $n+1$ original segments. For a set $S$ of
these segments, let $\Sigma(S)$ be their total length.

\begin{lemma}
If $S$ and $T$ are disjoint, distinct sets of original segments, then Xiang Yu can
make at most $n$ cuts so that
\[
 G\le \bigl|\Sigma(S)-\Sigma(T)\bigr|.
\]
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Assume $\Sigma(S)\ge\Sigma(T)>0$. Concatenate the segments in $S$ in any order
and do the same for $T$, aligning the two concatenations at their left endpoints.
On the common portion of length $\Sigma(T)$, Xiang Yu transfers every internal
boundary from either concatenation to the other one. The resulting subpieces on the
common portion occur in equal pairs.

Let $s=|S|$, $t=|T|$, and let $r=n+1-s-t$ be the number of original segments outside
$S\cup T$. Suppose the common portion meets $h$ segments of the $S$-concatenation.
Creating the matching partition uses at most
\[
 (h-1)+(t-1)+1=h+t-1
\]
cuts; the final $+1$ is needed only when the common portion ends inside an $S$-segment.
Xiang Yu bisects all $r$ outside segments. In the overhang, he leaves its first
component unpaired and bisects every later complete $S$-segment, using at most $s-h$
more cuts. Hence the total is at most
\[
 (h+t-1)+r+(s-h)=s+t+r-1=n.
\]
If the common portion ends at an existing boundary, or if the two totals are equal,
even fewer cuts are needed.

After these cuts, every piece length occurs in a pair except possibly the first
component of the overhang. Its length is at most $\Sigma(S)-\Sigma(T)$. In the
alternating sum defining $G$, all equal pairs cancel, leaving at most that unpaired
length. If $T=\varnothing$, Xiang Yu instead leaves one segment of $S$ unpaired and
bisects every other original segment; again at most $n$ cuts are used, and the same
bound follows.
\end{proof}

There are $2^{n+1}$ subset sums $\Sigma(S)$, all lying in $[0,1]$. After sorting them,
two consecutive sums differ by at most
\[
 \frac{1}{2^{n+1}-1}.
\]
Choose corresponding distinct subsets and delete their common elements. The resulting
sets are disjoint and still distinct, and their subset-sum difference is unchanged.
The lemma therefore gives $G\le\delta_n$.

\medskip
\noindent\textbf{Liu Bang can force $G\ge\delta_n$.}
He cuts the stick into lengths proportional to
\[
 1,2,4,\dots,2^n.
\]
Scale temporarily so that these lengths are exactly $1,2,\dots,2^n$; their total is
$2^{n+1}-1$. We prove that $G\ge1$.

For $1\le k\le n$, we claim
\[
 x_2+x_4+\cdots+x_{2k}
 \le 2^{n-1}+2^{n-2}+\cdots+2^{n-k}.
 \tag{2}
\]
Assume that (2) first fails at $k$. Its validity for $k-1$ then implies
$x_{2k}>2^{n-k}$. Hence each of the first $2k$ pieces is longer than $2^{n-k}$.
Such pieces can come only from the $k$ largest original segments
\[
 2^{n-k+1},2^{n-k+2},\dots,2^n.
\]
Because $x_{2i}\le x_{2i-1}$ for every $i$,
\[
 x_2+x_4+\cdots+x_{2k}
 \le \frac{x_1+x_2+\cdots+x_{2k}}2
 \le \frac{2^{n-k+1}+\cdots+2^n}{2},
\]
which is exactly the right-hand side of (2), a contradiction.

Taking $k=n$ in (2), Xiang Yu's total is at most
$1+2+\cdots+2^{n-1}=2^n-1$. Liu Bang therefore receives at least $2^n$, so
$G\ge1$ in the scaled game. Dividing by $2^{n+1}-1$ gives
$G\ge\delta_n$ in the unit-length game.

Thus the optimal score is
\[
 \frac{1+\delta_n}{2}
 =\frac12\left(1+\frac{1}{2^{n+1}-1}\right)
 =\frac{2^n}{2^{n+1}-1}.
\]

grading_scheme.title: Finding the optimal value
grading_scheme.desc: One point should be given for the correct answer $c_n=\frac{2^n}{2^{n+1}-1}$. This answer point may be awarded without a complete proof.

grading_scheme.title: Valuing the drafting game
grading_scheme.desc: For proving, for a sorted finite list $z_1\ge z_2\ge\cdots\ge z_m\ge0$, that taking a largest remaining piece is optimal and that the score difference for the player to move is $z_1-z_2+z_3-\cdots+(-1)^{m+1}z_m$. The solution must then connect this to $G=x_1-x_2+\cdots+x_{2n+1}$ and Liu Bang's score $(1+G)/2$.

grading_scheme.title: Proving Xiang Yu's upper bound
grading_scheme.desc: For proving $G\le 1/(2^{n+1}-1)$. Award $1$ point for a valid equal-pair/cutting lemma: for disjoint, distinct sets $S,T$ of the original segments, construct at most $n$ legal cuts so that equal pieces cancel and $G\le|\Sigma(S)-\Sigma(T)|$, with the endpoint, equal-total, and empty-set cases handled as needed. Award the second point for applying the pigeonhole principle to the $2^{n+1}$ subset sums in $[0,1]$, deleting common elements while preserving distinctness and the difference, and obtaining the stated upper bound.

grading_scheme.title: Choosing the powers-of-two division
grading_scheme.desc: For proposing Liu Bang's division in the ratio $1:2:4:\cdots:2^n$ and correctly reducing the desired lower bound to $G\ge1$ after scaling the total length to $2^{n+1}-1$.

grading_scheme.title: Proving Liu Bang's lower bound
grading_scheme.desc: For proving that the powers-of-two division always gives $G\ge1$. One standard route is to establish for every $k$ that $x_2+x_4+\cdots+x_{2k}\le 2^{n-1}+\cdots+2^{n-k}$ and then take $k=n$; the graph/$\Delta$ argument or another rigorous equivalent is also accepted. Award $1$ of the $2$ points for a correct key inequality or structural lemma with substantial justification but an incomplete finish, and both points for deriving the lower bound and hence the stated value of $c_n$.

problem:
Shan-Yu and Mulan are playing a game. Let $\theta$ be an angle with $0^\circ<\theta<180^\circ$, known to both players. Initially, Shan-Yu makes a paper triangle $T$ with measurements of his choice. They then repeat the following steps:
\begin{itemize}
\item If $T$ has an angle measuring exactly $\theta$, the game stops and Mulan wins.
\item Otherwise, Mulan chooses a nonvertex point $P$ on the perimeter of $T$ and makes a straight cut from $P$ to the opposite vertex, splitting $T$ into two triangles.
\item Shan-Yu discards one of the two triangles; the other becomes the new $T$.
\end{itemize}
For which real values of $\theta$ can Mulan guarantee victory after finitely many steps, no matter how Shan-Yu plays?
reference_solution:
The winning angles are exactly
\[
 \boxed{\theta=\frac{180^\circ}{n}\quad\text{for an integer }n\ge2.}
\]

First suppose $\theta=180^\circ/n$. We use the following observation: if the current
triangle has an angle $k\theta$ for a positive integer $k$, then Mulan can force a win.
This follows by induction on $k$. The case $k=1$ is immediate. For $k>1$, draw from
that vertex the unique interior ray that splits the angle into $\theta$ and
$(k-1)\theta$; it meets the opposite side at a nonvertex point. If Shan-Yu keeps the
first child, the angle $\theta$ is already present; if he keeps the second, apply the
induction hypothesis.

If the initial triangle does not already contain $\theta$, Mulan first cuts along an
altitude whose foot lies in the relative interior of the opposite side. Such an altitude
always exists, for example from a vertex of a largest angle. Whichever child Shan-Yu
keeps is a right triangle. Name it $ABC$ so that
\[
 \angle A=90^\circ,
 \qquad
 \angle B\le45^\circ.
\]
For $n=2$ this triangle already contains $\theta=90^\circ$. Assume $n\ge3$ and take
$k=\lfloor n/2\rfloor$. Since
\[
 \frac n4<\left\lfloor\frac n2\right\rfloor\le\frac n2,
\]
we have
\[
 45^\circ<k\theta\le90^\circ.
\]
Thus $0^\circ<k\theta-\angle B<90^\circ$, so there is a point $D$ in the interior of
$BC$ such that
\[
 \angle BAD=k\theta-\angle B.
\]
Then
\[
 \angle ADB=180^\circ-k\theta=(n-k)\theta,
 \qquad
 \angle ADC=k\theta.
\]
Thus either child retained by Shan-Yu has an angle that is a positive integral multiple
of $\theta$, and the observation finishes the winning strategy.

Conversely, suppose $\theta$ is not of the form $180^\circ/n$. Call an angle
\emph{safe} if it is not an integral multiple of $\theta$, and call a triangle safe if
all three of its angles are safe. In particular, $180^\circ$ is safe. We claim that a
safe triangle cannot be cut into two triangles that are both unsafe.

Indeed, suppose $D$ lies on $BC$ and $ABD$ is unsafe, while $ABC$ is safe. Since
$\angle B$ is safe, at least one of $\angle BAD$ and $\angle ADB$ must be an integral
multiple of $\theta$.
If $\angle BAD$ is such a multiple, then
\[
 \angle CDA=\angle B+\angle BAD,
 \qquad
 \angle DAC=\angle A-\angle BAD
\]
are both safe. If instead $\angle ADB$ is such a multiple, then
\[
 \angle CDA=180^\circ-\angle ADB,
 \qquad
 \angle DAC=\angle ADB-\angle C
\]
are both safe. In either case, adding or subtracting an integral multiple of $\theta$
cannot turn a safe angle into a multiple of $\theta$. The remaining angle $\angle C$
was already safe, so $ACD$ is safe. Therefore at least one child of every cut is safe.

Shan-Yu may start with an equilateral triangle. Its angles are safe, because
$60^\circ=k\theta$ would imply $\theta=180^\circ/(3k)$, contrary to the assumption.
After every cut he keeps a safe child. No retained triangle ever has an angle $\theta$,
so Mulan cannot force a win.

grading_scheme.title: Finding the winning angles
grading_scheme.desc: One point should be given for the correct classification $\theta=\frac{180^\circ}{n}$ for an integer $n\ge2$.

grading_scheme.title: Reducing a multiple of $\theta$ to $\theta$
grading_scheme.desc: For proving that if a current triangle has an angle $k\theta$ for some positive integer $k$, then Mulan can force a win, by cutting that angle into $\theta$ and $(k-1)\theta$ and applying induction.

grading_scheme.title: Forcing a suitable right triangle
grading_scheme.desc: For cutting along an altitude whose foot is inside the opposite side (for example, from a largest-angle vertex), so either retained child is a right triangle; labeling the smaller acute angle by $B\le45^\circ$; and, for $n\ge3$, taking $k=\lfloor n/2\rfloor$ to obtain $45^\circ<k\theta\le90^\circ$.

grading_scheme.title: Completing Mulan's winning strategy
grading_scheme.desc: For choosing the cut so that $\angle BAD=k\theta-\angle B$ and computing that the two possible retained children contain angles $(n-k)\theta$ and $k\theta$, respectively, thereby reducing both cases to the multiple-angle lemma. The case $n=2$ must also be covered.

grading_scheme.title: Proving the safe-child invariant
grading_scheme.desc: For the converse, assume $\theta$ is not of the stated form, note that $180^\circ$ is safe, define an angle to be safe when it is not an integral multiple of $\theta$, and prove that a triangle with three safe angles cannot be cut into two triangles that are both unsafe. Full credit requires checking the angle identities in the two possible cases for which a new angle is a multiple of $\theta$. Award $1$ of the $2$ points for identifying the correct invariant and giving a substantially correct but incomplete angle check.

grading_scheme.title: Giving Shan-Yu's counterstrategy
grading_scheme.desc: For starting with an equilateral triangle, proving that its $60^\circ$ angles are safe when $\theta$ is not of the form $180^\circ/n$, and having Shan-Yu retain a safe child after every cut, so that Mulan never reaches an angle equal to $\theta$.

reference_solution:
The complete family is
\[
 \boxed{f(x)=x+c\quad\text{for some constant }c\ge0.}
\]
Every such function works, because the required chain becomes
\[
 \sqrt{\frac{x^2+(y+c)^2}{2}}
 \ge \frac{x+y+c}{2}
 \ge \sqrt{x(y+c)},
\]
which is the quadratic-mean--arithmetic-mean--geometric-mean inequality for the
positive numbers $x$ and $y+c$.

We prove that there are no other functions. Let $P(x,y)$ denote the left inequality
and $Q(x,y)$ the right inequality. Substituting $(x,y)=(f(t),t)$, the two outer
expressions are both $f(t)$, so equality is forced in the middle:
\[
 \frac{f(f(t))+t}{2}=f(t).
 \tag{1}
\]
Thus $f(f(t))=2f(t)-t$. If $d(t):=f(t)-t$, then (1) says
$d(f(t))=d(t)$, and induction gives
\[
 f^m(t)=t+m d(t),
 \qquad m=0,1,2,\dots.
 \tag{2}
\]
All iterates are positive, so $d(t)\ge0$.

We next show that all positive values of $d(t)$ are equal. Choose $x,y>0$ with
$d(x)=A>0$ and $d(y)=B>0$. From (2),
\[
 f^m(x)=x+mA,
 \qquad
 f^n(y)=y+nB.
\]
Apply $Q$ to $f^m(x)$ and $f^n(y)$, and put
\[
 U=x+mA,
 \qquad
 V=y+(n+1)B.
\]
The resulting inequality is
\[
 \frac{U+V+A-B}{2}\ge\sqrt{UV},
\]
which is equivalent to
\[
 \left(\sqrt{x+mA}-\sqrt{y+(n+1)B}\right)^2\ge B-A.
 \tag{3}
\]
This holds for all nonnegative integers $m,n$. If $A<B$, set
$\varepsilon=\sqrt{B-A}$ and write
\[
 s_m=\sqrt{x+mA},
 \qquad
 t_n=\sqrt{y+(n+1)B}.
\]
The sequence $(t_n)$ is unbounded and $t_{n+1}-t_n\to0$. Choose $N$ so that
$t_{n+1}-t_n<\varepsilon$ for all $n\ge N$, and then choose $m$ with $s_m>t_N$.
For the index $n\ge N$ satisfying $t_n\le s_m<t_{n+1}$, at least one of
$|s_m-t_n|$ and $|s_m-t_{n+1}|$ is less than $\varepsilon$, contradicting (3).
Hence $A\ge B$. By symmetry, $B\ge A$, so $A=B$.

Therefore either $d(t)=0$ for every $t$, giving the identity function, or there is a
single constant $D>0$ such that
\[
 f(t)\in\{t,t+D\}
 \qquad(t>0).
 \tag{4}
\]
Assume the second case. If there is no fixed point, then $f(t)=t+D$ for all $t$ and
we are done. Otherwise choose a point $x$ with $f(x)=x+D$ and a fixed point $y$ with
$f(y)=y$. Necessarily $x\ne y$. If $x>y$, then $P(x,y)$ yields
\[
 \frac{x+y+D}{2}
 \le \sqrt{\frac{x^2+y^2}{2}}<x,
\]
so $x>y+D$. If $x<y$, then $Q(y,x)$ yields
\[
 \sqrt{y(x+D)}\le\frac{x+y}{2}<y,
\]
so $x<y-D$. In either case,
\[
 |x-y|>D.
 \tag{5}
\]
Consequently, whenever $f(x)=x+D$, every positive $t$ with $|t-x|\le D$ must also
satisfy $f(t)=t+D$. Given any $t>0$, subdivide the interval joining $x$ to $t$ into
finitely many steps of length at most $D$. Applying the preceding implication along
this chain shows successively that every subdivision point, and finally $t$, satisfies
$f(t)=t+D$. Hence $f(t)=t+D$ for all $t>0$.

Combining this with the identity case gives $f(x)=x+c$ for exactly the constants
$c\ge0$.

grading_scheme.title: Finding and checking the solution family
grading_scheme.desc: For identifying the complete family $f(x)=x+c$ with $c\ge0$ and verifying it by the quadratic-mean--arithmetic-mean--geometric-mean inequality applied to $x$ and $y+c$. The identity function $c=0$ must be included.

grading_scheme.title: Forcing the second-iterate relation
grading_scheme.desc: For substituting $(x,y)=(f(t),t)$, observing that the two outer expressions coincide, and deriving $f(f(t))=2f(t)-t$.

grading_scheme.title: Deriving arithmetic-progression iterates
grading_scheme.desc: For defining $d(t)=f(t)-t$, proving $f^m(t)=t+m d(t)$ for all $m\ge0$, and using positivity of all iterates to conclude $d(t)\ge0$.

grading_scheme.title: Showing all positive increments are equal
grading_scheme.desc: For proving that if $d(x)=A>0$ and $d(y)=B>0$, then $A=B$. In the reference route, applying the right inequality to iterates yields $(\sqrt{x+mA}-\sqrt{y+(n+1)B})^2\ge B-A$; a bracketing argument with the vanishing gaps of the square-root sequence rules out $A<B$, and symmetry finishes. Award $1$ of the $2$ points for deriving a correct decisive inequality or equivalent comparison with a substantial but incomplete equality argument.

grading_scheme.title: Separating fixed and shifted points
grading_scheme.desc: For reducing to $f(t)\in\{t,t+D\}$ for a fixed $D>0$ and proving that a shifted point $x$ and a fixed point $y$ must satisfy $|x-y|>D$, using the appropriate one of the two original inequalities.

grading_scheme.title: Eliminating mixed behavior
grading_scheme.desc: For using the separation result to show that every point within distance $D$ of a shifted point is also shifted, propagating through overlapping intervals to all of $\mathbb{R}_{>0}$, and concluding that the function is either the identity or $f(x)=x+D$ everywhere.

problem:
Let $a_1,a_2,a_3,\dots$ be an infinite sequence of positive integers greater than $1$. Suppose that, for every positive integer $n$, the number $a_{n+1}$ is the smallest positive integer greater than $a_n$ such that
\[
  \gcd(a_{n+1},a_i)>1
  \qquad\text{for every }i=1,2,\dots,n.
\]
Prove that there exist positive integers $T$ and $L$ such that
\[
  a_{n+T}=a_n+L
\]
for every positive integer $n$.
reference_solution:
For a positive integer $u$, let $\operatorname{rad}(u)$ be the product of the distinct primes dividing
$u$. Define a relation among terms of the sequence by
\[
 a_m\prec a_n
 \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
 m<n\ \text{ and }\ \operatorname{rad}(a_m)\mid\operatorname{rad}(a_n).
\]
If $a_m\prec a_n$, then the condition of sharing a prime with $a_n$ is redundant: any
integer sharing a prime with $a_m$ automatically shares one with $a_n$. Every
nonminimal term has, after finitely many descents in the index, a $\prec$-minimal
predecessor whose radical divides its own. Consequently, an integer $x\ge a_1$ belongs
to the sequence if and only if it has gcd greater than $1$ with every $\prec$-minimal
term smaller than $x$.

Necessity is immediate. Conversely, this condition makes $x$ share a prime with every
term of the sequence smaller than $x$. Hence, while the current sequence term is below
$x$, the integer $x$ is an admissible candidate for the next term. The sequence cannot
jump past $x$, and only finitely many integers lie below $x$, so it must eventually
select $x$.

Call a prime \emph{large} if it exceeds $a_1^2$. The key point is that no
$\prec$-minimal term is divisible by a large prime.

\begin{lemma}
If $a_n$ is divisible by a prime $p>a_1^2$, then $a_n$ is not $\prec$-minimal.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
We argue by induction on $n$. Write $a_n=pc$. The case $n=1$ is impossible, since
every prime divisor of $a_1$ is at most $a_1$. For $n>1$, choose a prime $q$ dividing
$\gcd(a_1,a_n)$; such a prime exists by the definition of the sequence. Let $k$ be
the least nonnegative integer for which $q^k c\ge a_1$, and set $b=q^k c$.
If $k=0$, then $b=c<a_n$. If $k\ge1$, minimality of $k$ gives
$q^{k-1}c<a_1$, and hence
\[
 b=q^kc<q a_1\le a_1^2<p\le pc=a_n.
\]
Thus in every case
\[
 b\in[a_1,a_n).
\]

Consider any $\prec$-minimal earlier term $a_i<a_n$. By the induction hypothesis,
$p\nmid a_i$. Since $\gcd(a_n,a_i)>1$, some common prime divisor of $a_n$ and $a_i$
is different from $p$ and therefore divides $c$. Hence $\gcd(b,a_i)>1$.

Thus $b$ satisfies all conditions imposed by the $\prec$-minimal predecessors, so the
criterion above shows that $b$ occurs in the sequence. Moreover every prime divisor of
$b=q^kc$ divides $a_n=pc$, because $q\mid a_n$. Hence
$\operatorname{rad}(b)\mid\operatorname{rad}(a_n)$ and $b<a_n$, so $b\prec a_n$. Therefore $a_n$ is not minimal.
\end{proof}

Let $\mathcal P$ be the finite set of primes at most $a_1^2$, and define
\[
 S(a)=\{p\in\mathcal P:p\mid a\},
 \qquad
 \mathcal F=\{S(a_n):n\ge1\}.
\]
We claim that the sequence consists exactly of the integers $a\ge a_1$ satisfying
$S(a)\in\mathcal F$.

One direction is immediate. Conversely, suppose $S(a)=S(a_n)$ for some $n$.
Any two terms of the sequence have gcd greater than $1$, because the later term was
chosen to share a prime with the earlier one. Let $a_i$ be a $\prec$-minimal term.
By the lemma, all prime divisors of $a_i$ lie in $\mathcal P$. Choose a common prime
divisor of $a_i$ and $a_n$; it lies in $S(a_n)=S(a)$, so it also divides $a$. Thus $a$
shares a prime with every minimal term, and the criterion above shows that $a$ occurs
in the sequence.

Now set
\[
 L=\prod_{p\in\mathcal P}p.
\]
The set $S(a)$ depends only on the residue class of $a$ modulo $L$. Hence membership
in the sequence, among integers $a\ge a_1$, is periodic modulo $L$. Let
\[
 \mathcal R=\{r\in\mathbb Z/L\mathbb Z:S(a)\in\mathcal F
             \text{ for }a\equiv r\pmod L\},
 \qquad T=|\mathcal R|.
\]
In the interval $(a_n,a_n+L]$ there is exactly one representative of each class in
$\mathcal R$, and no representative of any other class belongs to the sequence.
Thus this interval contains exactly the next $T$ terms, with $a_n+L$ as the last one.
Consequently
\[
 a_{n+T}=a_n+L
\]
for every $n\ge1$.

grading_scheme.title: Reducing to radical-minimal terms
grading_scheme.desc: For introducing a redundancy relation such as $a_m\prec a_n$ when $m<n$ and $\operatorname{rad}(a_m)\mid\operatorname{rad}(a_n)$, and proving that an integer $x\ge a_1$ occurs in the sequence exactly when it shares a nontrivial divisor with every $\prec$-minimal earlier term below $x$.

grading_scheme.title: Proving that large primes do not occur in minimal terms
grading_scheme.desc: For proving the key lemma that a $\prec$-minimal term cannot be divisible by a prime $p>a_1^2$. The $3$ points may be awarded as follows: (i) construct $b\in[a_1,a_n)$ from $a_n=pc$ and a prime $q\mid\gcd(a_1,a_n)$ (for example, take $b=c$ if $c\ge a_1$, and otherwise the least $q^k c\ge a_1$) and justify $b<a_n$; (ii) use induction to show that $p$ divides no minimal predecessor, hence $b$ satisfies all nonredundant gcd conditions and therefore occurs in the sequence; (iii) prove $\operatorname{rad}(b)\mid\operatorname{rad}(a_n)$ and $b<a_n$, contradicting minimality. A correct equivalent finite-prime lemma receives the same credit.

grading_scheme.title: Classifying terms by small-prime signatures
grading_scheme.desc: For letting $\mathcal P$ be the finite set of primes at most $a_1^2$, defining $S(a)=\{p\in\mathcal P:p\mid a\}$ and $\mathcal F=\{S(a_n):n\ge1\}$, and proving the exact characterization $a$ occurs if and only if $a\ge a_1$ and $S(a)\in\mathcal F$. Award $1$ of the $2$ points for showing that only finitely many primes matter or that membership depends on $S(a)$, but not proving the converse characterization.

grading_scheme.title: Deducing periodicity and the translation law
grading_scheme.desc: For taking $L=\prod_{p\in\mathcal P}p$, observing that sequence membership is periodic modulo $L$, letting $T$ be the number of occurring residue classes, and proving that the $T$-th next term after $a_n$ is exactly $a_n+L$, so $a_{n+T}=a_n+L$ for every $n$.
